mappings.rationaleAI: X-ray diffraction crystallography determining atomic arrangements in crystalline solids, Bragg's law, and synchrotron techniques directly address crystal patterns (A.1) and diffraction methods (A.2).
Electron band theory, conductors versus insulators versus semiconductors, doping, p-n junctions, and transistors comprehensively address electronic structure of solids (B.5) and semiconductors (E).
Glass as an amorphous solid lacking crystalline order, the glass transition temperature, and condensed matter physics of disordered materials directly address the glassy/amorphous state (F).
Superconductivity in metals covering transition temperature, BCS theory (Cooper pairs), the Meissner effect, and high-temperature superconductors directly addresses superconductivity in metals (D.8).

Magnetic materials and domains, quantum mechanical explanations of magnetic behaviour, and Earth's magnetic field address principal types of magnetic behaviour in solids (B.6) and magnetic phenomena in metals (D.9).
States of matter covering solids and phase transitions addresses the classification of solids according to electronic structure and bonding (B.1).

The photoelectric effect, photon interactions with matter, and photovoltaic applications address the interaction of light with solids (B.7), the photoelectric effect (B.7.c), and optical effects in semiconductors (E.4.a).

Electron behaviour in solids including band theory, conduction, and quantum tunneling addresses the nature and mobility of electrons in conductors/insulators/semiconductors (B.5.a) and mechanisms of conduction (E.2).

Low-temperature phenomena including superconductivity, superfluidity, and Bose-Einstein condensation relate to effects of temperature on crystal strength and hardness (A.5) through extreme-condition material behaviour.
